topic_name: (MAM) MODERATE ACUTE MALNUTRITION AND SUPPLEMENTARY FEEDING

subtopics.subtopic_title: CLINIC-READY DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA AND MISCLASSIFICATION PITFALLS
subtopics.category: DEFINING MODERATE ACUTE MALNUTRITION IN INDIAN CLINICS
subtopics.concept: Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in children aged 6–59 months is defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) as weight-for-height/length (WHZ/WLZ) between -2 and -3 z-scores and/or mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) between 115–124 mm, without bilateral pitting oedema. In Indian clinics, the operational definition emphasizes moderate wasting by either metric, clinical stability with appetite, and the absence of acute danger signs or complications requiring inpatient care. It is critical to document the metric used, as program eligibility often depends on it. Importantly, MAM should not be equated with “underweight” (low weight-for-age), which may indicate chronic stunting rather than acute malnutrition. To check for oedema, press both feet for several seconds and observe for persistent pitting. Borderline measurements should be repeated, and findings should be triangulated with the clinical picture, including recent weight loss, poor intake, and frequent infections. Explicitly label the child as having uncomplicated MAM or wasting with complications, as the intensity of follow-up and thresholds for escalation differ. Indian facility-based malnutrition guidelines describe MAM as 70–80% of median weight-for-height (equivalent to z-scores between -3 and -2) or MUAC 115–125 mm, without oedema, in clinically well children with appetite, managed on an outpatient basis when supplementary feeding is available. However, some manuals contain unit errors; MUAC values should always be interpreted in millimetres to align with WHO thresholds. A practical bedside approach involves a one-minute wasting screen during every sick-child visit. This includes assessing for visible wasting, checking for oedema, measuring MUAC (or weight-for-height/length if feasible), and inquiring about appetite and recent weight loss. Such proactive screening helps prevent delayed recognition of MAM, which could otherwise progress to severe acute malnutrition (SAM) or lead to complications. Not specified in the source are the sensitivity and specificity of diagnostic criteria, sampling pitfalls, or additional interpretation dos and don’ts. Accurate identification and classification of MAM are essential to ensure timely and appropriate management.

subtopics.subtopic_title: POPULATION BURDEN AND CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE DETERMINANTS
subtopics.category: BURDEN AND DRIVERS OF ACUTE WASTING IN INDIA
subtopics.concept: Wasting remains a significant public health concern among children under five in India, with the NFHS-5 (2019–2021) reporting a prevalence of 19.3% for wasting (weight-for-height < -2) and 7.7% for severe wasting (< -3). While “moderate wasting” is not explicitly listed as an indicator, it can be estimated at 11.6% by subtracting the severely wasted proportion from the total wasted population. This is clinically important because moderate wasting constitutes the majority of cases and represents a critical opportunity for outpatient care to prevent progression to severe wasting and associated mortality. Key determinants of wasting in India include inadequate diet quality or quantity, inappropriate feeding practices, recurrent infections such as diarrhoea and respiratory illnesses, and suboptimal water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) conditions. These factors interact bidirectionally, as infections suppress appetite and increase metabolic demands, while undernutrition weakens immunity, exacerbating illness severity. Clinicians are advised not to attribute wasting solely to poverty but to identify and document two likely drivers for each child diagnosed with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM): one dietary factor (e.g., low energy density, inadequate meal frequency, or milk displacement) and one health-related factor (e.g., recurrent diarrhoea, suspected tuberculosis, chronic cough, or anaemia). Management plans should address both drivers simultaneously through nutritional rehabilitation and active treatment of underlying illnesses. Evidence from NITI Aayog and UNICEF underscores that wasting is highly sensitive to short-term shocks such as food insecurity and seasonal peaks in infections, necessitating integrated prevention and treatment strategies across community and healthcare systems. Acute wasting should be treated as a sentinel event, prompting screening for food access challenges, caregiving constraints, and treatable diseases, particularly when symptoms are persistent or atypical. Generic dietary advice without addressing the illness–malnutrition cycle or caregiver limitations is unlikely to yield meaningful improvements in outcomes.

subtopics.subtopic_title: INFECTION CATABOLISM AND GUT DYSFUNCTION IN WASTING
subtopics.category: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF WASTING AND CLINICAL CORRELATES
subtopics.concept: Acute wasting in children arises from an imbalance between dietary intake or nutrient absorption and the body’s physiological demands, exacerbated by the infection–nutrition cycle. Intercurrent infections elevate energy expenditure and drive inflammatory catabolism, while anorexia and feeding disruptions reduce nutrient intake. Concurrent micronutrient deficiencies further impair immune function, increasing susceptibility to recurrent infections. Clinically, persistent poor appetite in a child should be interpreted as a marker of unresolved illness or inflammation rather than a behavioural issue, warranting morbidity screening and closer follow-up. In resource-limited settings, gut dysfunction significantly hinders recovery. Environmental enteric dysfunction (EED), a subclinical enteropathy caused by repeated exposure to enteric pathogens, leads to intestinal inflammation, impaired nutrient absorption, and compromised gut barrier integrity. Affected children may exhibit fluctuating appetite, frequent minor infections, and poor weight gain despite seemingly adequate caloric intake. Management should incorporate counselling on WASH (water, sanitation, and hygiene) practices and prompt treatment of diarrhoea to mitigate ongoing gut injury. Recovery from moderate wasting in children aged 6–59 months necessitates a planned caloric “top-up” beyond usual requirements. According to the 2023 WHO guideline, total daily energy needs during recovery are estimated at 100–130 kcal/kg/day. Nutritional supplements, such as take-home rations (THR), home-based complementary meals (HCM), or ready-to-use supplementary foods (RUSF) where available, should complement rather than replace family-provided meals. Relapse within weeks of discharge often signals unresolved infection burden, food insecurity, or caregiving challenges. Such relapses should prompt a reassessment of underlying drivers and intensified follow-up rather than relying solely on repeated dietary advice. Addressing these multifactorial contributors is essential for sustained recovery and prevention of recurrent wasting.

case_studies.title: Moderate Acute Malnutrition with Persistent Poor Appetite and Recurrent Infections
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural Indian village presents to a primary health center with his mother, who reports poor appetite and no weight gain over the past 2 months. He has had two episodes of watery diarrhoea in the past month, each lasting 3–4 days, and a low-grade fever for the past week. His family relies on untreated well water and open defecation is common in the area. The child was previously breastfed but now receives a mix of family foods and cow's milk. On examination, he appears thin with visible wasting but no oedema. His weight-for-height z-score is -2.5. The child is lethargic and uninterested in feeding during the visit. He has a normal respiratory rate, no signs of dehydration, and no focal infections on examination. His mother reports that he has been irritable and less active than usual. A mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) measurement is 11.2 cm.
case_studies.learning_objective: To assess the impact of infection-related catabolism and gut dysfunction on recovery from moderate acute malnutrition, and to determine when further evaluation or admission is needed for persistent poor appetite and recurrent infections.

subtopics.subtopic_title: EMERGENCY AND DANGER SIGNS GUIDING ADMISSION OR REFERRAL
subtopics.category: TRIAGE AND DISPOSITION FOR ACUTE WASTING
subtopics.concept:
In the context of moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) and supplementary feeding, triage and disposition for acute wasting should prioritize identifying emergency and danger signs over anthropometric measurements. While confirming acute wasting criteria, both MUAC and WHZ/WLZ should be recorded when feasible, as program entry metrics vary across India. Facility-based paediatric triage logic, such as ETAT, should then be applied. Emergency signs necessitating immediate treatment and typically admission or urgent referral include not breathing or gasping, obstructed breathing, central cyanosis, severe respiratory distress (manifesting as grunting, head nodding, severe lower chest indrawing, apnoeic spells, stridor in a calm child, or inability to feed due to respiratory distress), shock (characterized by cold hands, capillary refill time >3 seconds, and a weak, fast pulse), coma on AVPU, ongoing convulsions, and severe dehydration in diarrhoea (indicated by diarrhoea plus at least two of the following: lethargy, sunken eyes, or a very slow skin pinch). Any child presenting with these signs should be stabilized immediately and not categorized as “uncomplicated.”

Additionally, general danger signs as outlined in IMNCI/IMCI should be screened, including inability to drink or breastfeed, vomiting everything, convulsions, and lethargy or unconsciousness. Admission or referral as “complicated wasting” is warranted if any of these signs or complications are present, such as severe pneumonia, temperature instability (fever ≥39°C or hypothermia <35°C), suspected hypoglycaemia, severe palmar pallor, extensive infection requiring intramuscular antibiotics, or a caregiver’s inability to provide safe home care. Initial laboratory investigations, stabilization steps, thresholds for PICU admission, and discharge or review triggers are not specified in the source. The presence of any emergency or danger sign underscores the critical need for prompt intervention and appropriate referral to ensure the child’s survival and recovery.

case_studies.title: Moderate Acute Malnutrition with Danger Signs
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural village in India is brought to the primary health center by his mother. She reports that he has had watery diarrhea for the past 4 days and has been refusing to breastfeed or drink fluids for the last 24 hours. On arrival, the child appears lethargic and unresponsive to stimuli. His mother mentions he has been unusually drowsy since yesterday. On examination, his weight-for-height z-score (WHZ) is -2.5, and his mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) measures 11.2 cm. He has sunken eyes, cold hands, and a capillary refill time of 4 seconds. The skin pinch test shows very slow skin recoil (>2 seconds). Respiratory examination reveals no grunting or stridor, but he has severe lower chest indrawing. His axillary temperature is 39.2°C. The mother expresses concern that she cannot manage his care at home due to her responsibilities with three other young children.
case_studies.learning_objective: Identify emergency and danger signs in a child with moderate acute malnutrition that warrant immediate stabilization, admission, or referral.

case_studies.title: Moderate Acute Malnutrition with Danger Signs in a 2-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy from a rural village in India presents to the outpatient department with a 4-day history of fever, decreased appetite, and lethargy. His mother reports he has been unable to breastfeed or drink water since the previous evening and has vomited everything offered. He has had loose stools for the past two days. On examination, the child appears drowsy and irritable. His weight-for-height z-score (WHZ) is -2.8, and his mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) is 11.7 cm. He has sunken eyes, a capillary refill time of 4 seconds, and cold extremities. His respiratory rate is 48 breaths per minute, with severe lower chest indrawing. The skin pinch test is very slow, taking over 3 seconds to return to normal. No convulsions are noted during observation. The caregiver expresses concerns about managing the child at home due to poor access to resources and transport.
case_studies.learning_objective: Identify emergency and danger signs in a child with moderate acute malnutrition that necessitate immediate stabilization, admission, or urgent referral.

subtopics.subtopic_title: HYDRATION ASSESSMENT AND ORAL REHYDRATION PLANS
subtopics.category: TRIAGE AND DISPOSITION FOR ACUTE WASTING
subtopics.concept: Hydration assessment and oral rehydration planning in children aged 6–59 months with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) requires careful adherence to paediatric-specific guidelines to avoid errors in fluid management. The 2023 WHO wasting guideline recommends using low-osmolarity oral rehydration solution (ORS) for children with MAM and dehydration but not shock, aligning with standard diarrhoea protocols for all children. The malnutrition-specific formulation, ReSoMal, is reserved exclusively for cases of severe wasting and/or nutritional oedema. For children meeting MAM criteria (no oedema and not severely wasted), usual dehydration management plans should be followed, but with a lower threshold for observation or admission when oral intake is poor or caregivers cannot ensure reliable home care. In practical bedside decision-making, the approach should be tailored to the degree of dehydration. If no dehydration is present, feeding should continue, extra fluids should be provided after each loose stool, and early follow-up should be arranged, as acute illness can lead to rapid deterioration. For children with some dehydration—characterized by signs such as restlessness or irritability, sunken eyes, drinking eagerly, and a slow skin pinch—oral rehydration therapy (ORT) should be administered under observation when possible, with frequent reassessment and resumption of feeding as soon as it is tolerated. In cases of severe dehydration, indicated by diarrhoea plus at least two of the following: lethargy, sunken eyes, or a very slow skin pinch, or if any signs of shock are present, emergency treatment following standard protocols is required, with urgent admission or referral. A critical operational rule for residents is to manage dehydration in children with MAM as in any other child, unless severe wasting or oedema is present. Importantly, the malnutrition label should never delay emergency stabilization or disposition decisions, as timely intervention is paramount.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MEASUREMENT TECHNIQUE FOR MUAC LENGTH AND OEDEMA
subtopics.category: ANTHROPOMETRY AND BORDERLINE INTERPRETATION IN OUTPATIENT CARE
subtopics.concept: Accurate anthropometric measurement is a critical clinical skill in paediatric care, as it informs the diagnosis, risk stratification, eligibility for supplementary feeding, and follow-up intensity in children with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM). For measuring mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC), a non-stretchable tape should be used on the left arm. The midpoint between the acromion and olecranon must be identified, and the arm should remain relaxed during measurement. The tape should be wrapped snugly without compressing soft tissues, and the reading must be taken to the nearest millimetre. To ensure reliability, the measurement should be repeated at least twice. Oedema assessment requires a systematic approach. Bilateral pitting oedema should be checked by pressing on both feet for several seconds and confirming persistent pitting. Unilateral swelling, indicative of local pathology, should not be misclassified as nutritional oedema. Small errors in measurement can significantly impact classification across MAM thresholds; therefore, borderline values must be repeated, and the final confirmed measurement, along with the tool used, should be documented. In India, local program entry criteria may vary, with some community workflows prioritizing weight-for-height z-scores (WHZ) in conjunction with oedema over MUAC alone. To minimize exclusion due to administrative discrepancies, it is recommended to record both MUAC and WHZ or weight-for-length z-scores (WLZ) when feasible, along with an indication of whether measurements were repeated. Standardizing measurement tools and techniques within clinics is essential to prevent systematic misclassification, which could lead to inappropriate discharge or missed escalation of care. In cases where measurement quality is uncertain, clinicians should err on the side of caution by treating high-risk children with borderline values as needing intensified counselling, closer follow-up, and robust program linkage, rather than downgrading risk based on a potentially imprecise reading. This approach ensures that no child is inadvertently excluded from necessary care due to measurement errors or administrative ambiguity.

case_studies.title: Assessment of MUAC and Oedema in a Child with Suspected Malnutrition
case_studies.vignette: A 3-year-old girl is brought to a rural health clinic in India by her mother, who reports that the child has been eating poorly for the past month and appears increasingly fatigued. The mother notes that the child’s legs have been swelling for the last two weeks. On examination, the child is quiet and appears thin, with visible muscle wasting in the upper arms. The healthcare worker measures the mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) using a non-stretchable tape, ensuring the tape is placed at the midpoint between the acromion and olecranon on the left arm. The arm is relaxed during the measurement, and the reading is repeated twice to confirm accuracy. The MUAC is recorded as 11.4 cm. Bilateral pitting oedema is assessed by pressing firmly on both feet for 10 seconds; persistent pitting is noted. Weight-for-height z-scores are unavailable due to logistical constraints. The healthcare worker documents the findings and considers the next steps.
case_studies.learning_objective: Apply proper technique for MUAC measurement and oedema assessment to determine malnutrition severity and eligibility for supplementary feeding programs.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MANAGEMENT OF BORDERLINE VALUES AND DISCORDANT METRICS
subtopics.category: ANTHROPOMETRY AND BORDERLINE INTERPRETATION IN OUTPATIENT CARE
subtopics.concept: Management of borderline values and discordant metrics in children with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) requires careful evaluation and tailored decision-making. Borderline anthropometric values, such as mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) of 124–125 mm or weight-for-height z-score (WHZ) near -2, and discordance between MUAC and WHZ are common in outpatient care. This discordance arises because MUAC and WHZ identify overlapping but distinct risk phenotypes, with the highest mortality risk observed when both measures are low. The initial step in addressing discordant classifications is technical: repeat the measurements, confirm that equipment is calibrated, ensure proper length/height measurement techniques, and assess the child when calm to minimize variability. Once technical accuracy is confirmed, the more severe metric should guide clinical risk stratification and follow-up intensity. For program eligibility, the locally mandated entry metric should be used, but discordance must be explicitly documented to avoid underestimating clinical risk in cases where a child is deemed “not eligible” based on the preferred measure. WHO guidelines recommend prioritizing children with specific risk factors for intensive dietary support and closer follow-up. These risk factors include MUAC between 115–119 mm, weight-for-age z-score (WAZ) below -3, age under 24 months, failure to recover after prior interventions, relapse or history of severe wasting, comorbidities such as HIV, tuberculosis, or disabilities, and severe adverse social circumstances. In settings without access to specially formulated foods, these indicators should prompt tighter follow-up, a lower threshold for further investigation, and stronger linkage to supplementary feeding programs. When uncertainty persists, the safest approach is to manage the child as though they are eligible for intensified support. This includes scheduling earlier follow-up visits, closely monitoring dietary intake and morbidity, and ensuring that the family can access and appropriately use supplementary feeding resources without displacement or sharing. These steps are critical to mitigating the risk of progression to severe malnutrition and associated complications in vulnerable paediatric populations.

subtopics.subtopic_title: STRUCTURED OUTPATIENT HISTORY EXAMINATION AND FEEDING OBSERVATION
subtopics.category: CLINICAL SYNDROME RECOGNITION AND BEDSIDE ASSESSMENT IN MODERATE WASTING
subtopics.concept: Moderate wasting should be approached as a clinical syndrome rather than solely an anthropometric classification. During the initial minutes of an outpatient visit, clinicians must confirm three key disposition drivers: current appetite, caregiver-reported recent weight loss or declining mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC), and the presence of emergency or general danger signs as outlined in ETAT/IMNCI protocols. If the child is stable, the history should be extended to assess feeding function, including identifying who feeds the child, meal duration (prolonged meals may indicate fatigue, oral-motor difficulties, coercive feeding, or poor technique), patterns of food refusal, vomiting, and any coughing or choking during feeds. It is essential to screen for illness-related drivers of catabolism, such as diarrhoea, fever, cough or rapid breathing, ear discharge, and skin infections, as well as contextual factors like food insecurity, ration access, caregiver workload, or maternal illness and depression. The physical examination should prioritize identifying early complications that may alter follow-up intensity. These include hydration status, respiratory rate and effort, temperature, pallor, and bilateral pitting oedema. Visible loss of subcutaneous fat or reduced mid-arm muscle bulk should be documented as a baseline. Clinicians should specifically examine for signs of micronutrient deficiencies, such as periorificial dermatitis, angular cheilitis, glossitis, xerosis, or Bitot spots, and features of rickets that could impair feeding endurance, including swollen wrists, rachitic rosary, and delayed developmental milestones. Abdominal distension or hepatosplenomegaly warrants further evaluation for chronic infections, tuberculosis, or malabsorption. At least once during the baseline assessment, a real feeding session or simulated meal should be observed. This observation helps detect unsafe swallowing, inappropriate food textures, and poor caregiver-child interactions that may not be captured in a 24-hour dietary recall. Furthermore, it facilitates the development of a concrete, actionable “one change” plan to be implemented by the caregiver before the next visit. This structured approach ensures a comprehensive evaluation of moderate wasting and informs tailored interventions for effective management.

case_studies.title: Moderate Acute Malnutrition and Feeding Observation in a 2-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy is brought to a rural outpatient clinic in India by his mother, who reports that he has been losing weight over the past month despite eating 'normally.' She mentions that he has been refusing food more often and takes over an hour to finish meals, frequently coughing during feeding. He has had intermittent fever and a persistent cough for the last two weeks. The family relies on government ration supplies, which have been irregular due to local disruptions. On examination, the child appears thin with visible loss of subcutaneous fat, mild pallor, and a respiratory rate of 38 breaths per minute. There is no bilateral pitting oedema, but the mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) measures 11.3 cm, showing a decline from a prior measurement of 12 cm noted in his health card. No signs of dehydration or emergency danger signs are observed. Feeding observation reveals fatigue and occasional gagging during spoon-feeding. The mother appears visibly distressed and mentions difficulty managing household chores while caring for her three children.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess triage thresholds and feeding-related complications in moderate acute malnutrition using structured outpatient history and physical examination.

case_studies.title: Structured Outpatient Assessment in a Child with Moderate Acute Malnutrition
case_studies.vignette: A 22-month-old boy is brought to a rural outpatient clinic in India by his mother, who reports that he has been losing weight over the past month. She notes that his appetite has decreased, and he often refuses meals, taking nearly an hour to finish small portions. She also mentions that he has had intermittent diarrhoea and a low-grade fever for the past two weeks. The family struggles with food insecurity, relying on government rations, which have been inconsistent recently. On examination, the child appears thin with visible loss of subcutaneous fat and reduced mid-arm muscle bulk. His weight-for-height z-score is -2.5 SD, and his MUAC is 11.3 cm. He is alert but has mild pallor and xerosis of the skin. There is no bilateral pitting oedema or respiratory distress. Feeding observation reveals frequent food refusal and coughing during attempts to feed semi-solid food. His abdomen is soft but mildly distended without organomegaly. The mother appears fatigued and admits to feeling overwhelmed due to her workload and lack of support.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess triage thresholds and feeding observation in moderate acute malnutrition, identifying key drivers for disposition and follow-up intensity.

case_studies.title: Moderate Acute Malnutrition in a 2-Year-Old with Feeding Challenges
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the outpatient clinic in a rural district hospital by his mother, who reports that he has been eating poorly for the past month. She notes that he often refuses food, takes over an hour to finish a meal, and occasionally coughs while eating. He has had intermittent fever and diarrhea over the past two weeks, and she feels his arm has 'thinned down.' His mother reports no food ration access at home due to recent flooding, and she is struggling to care for him while managing her own chronic back pain. On examination, the child is alert but appears fatigued. His weight-for-height z-score is -2.3 SD, and his mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) is measured at 12.3 cm. There is visible loss of subcutaneous fat over his thighs and arms. He has mild pallor, angular cheilitis, and dry skin with patches of xerosis. Respiratory rate is 34 breaths per minute, with no chest retractions. Abdomen is soft but distended, and there is no hepatosplenomegaly. Feeding observation shows prolonged chewing and occasional coughing with semi-solid food.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess triage thresholds and feeding dynamics in moderate acute malnutrition using structured outpatient history and physical examination.

subtopics.subtopic_title: THERAPEUTIC GOALS AND INDICATIONS FOR SUPPLEMENTATION
subtopics.category: COUNSELLING AND HOME DIET THERAPY IN UNCOMPLICATED MODERATE WASTING
subtopics.concept: In the management of uncomplicated moderate acute malnutrition (MAM), the therapeutic goals are to achieve recovery from wasting, prevent progression to severe acute malnutrition (SAM), support neurodevelopment, and reduce the recurrence of infections. Counselling is central to this approach, as the World Health Organization (WHO) emphasizes the importance of a nutrient-dense diet alongside the identification and treatment of medical and psychosocial contributors to malnutrition. Supplementation should be risk-stratified rather than universally applied. In routine, non-humanitarian settings, WHO guidelines prioritize counselling and nutrient-dense home diets as the first-line intervention. Specially formulated supplements are selectively recommended for children at higher risk of poor outcomes, such as those with a mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) of 115–119 mm, weight-for-age z-score (WAZ) below -3, age under 24 months, relapse, comorbidities, disabilities, or severe social adversity. In high-risk humanitarian contexts, supplementation may be considered for all children aged 6–59 months with moderate wasting, alongside counselling and household food support. When supplementation is indicated, WHO conditionally prefers lipid-based nutrient supplements (LNS). If LNS is unavailable, improved fortified blended foods are recommended over unimproved blends. The supplement should provide 40–60% of the child’s total daily energy needs during recovery, with total energy requirements typically estimated at 100–130 kcal/kg/day. Importantly, supplementation should act as a dietary top-up and must not displace family foods. In Indian outpatient settings, practical challenges often revolve around whether families can reliably access and utilize supplements. Therefore, eligibility decisions should be paired with a concrete linkage plan and a follow-up schedule to monitor for potential issues such as food displacement, sharing, or clinical deterioration. Not specified in the source are alternative regimens for allergy or intolerance, protocols for multidrug-resistant (MDR) or extensively drug-resistant (XDR) malnutrition, and specific details on monitoring and adverse effects.

case_studies.title: Moderate Acute Malnutrition in a Toddler: Assessing Supplementation Needs
case_studies.vignette: A 20-month-old girl is brought to a rural health center in India by her mother, who reports poor appetite and weight loss over the past two months. The child has been more irritable and less active than usual, with occasional loose stools but no fever. The family struggles with food insecurity, relying on seasonal agricultural work. On examination, the child appears thin but alert, with a weight-for-age z-score of -3.2 and a mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) of 117 mm. Her skin is intact, and there is no edema. The mother has tried feeding her rice and lentils, but the child often refuses. A focused nutritional history reveals inadequate protein and micronutrient intake. There is no history of severe illness or hospitalization. The health center has access to locally produced lipid-based nutrient supplements (LNS) and fortified blended foods.
case_studies.learning_objective: Determine therapeutic goals and indications for supplementation in moderate acute malnutrition, including risk stratification and prioritization of nutrient-dense diets.

case_studies.title: Moderate Acute Malnutrition in a 14-Month-Old Child
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old girl is brought to a rural health center in India by her mother, who reports poor weight gain over the past 3 months. The child has had recurrent episodes of diarrhea and frequent respiratory infections. Her diet primarily consists of diluted cow's milk, rice, and occasional vegetables. The mother mentions financial difficulties and limited access to diverse foods. On examination, the child appears thin, with visible wasting of upper arms and thighs. Her mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) measures 117 mm, and her weight-for-age z-score (WAZ) is -3.2. She is alert but irritable, with no signs of dehydration or edema. There is no fever, and her respiratory rate is within normal limits. The family lives in a flood-prone area with limited access to clean water and sanitation. The mother expresses concern about her child’s frequent illnesses and asks for advice on improving her nutrition.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess indications for supplementary feeding in moderate acute malnutrition and determine therapeutic goals based on risk factors.

subtopics.subtopic_title: QUANTIFIED MEAL FREQUENCY ENERGY DENSITY AND PROTEIN ANCHORS
subtopics.category: COUNSELLING AND HOME DIET THERAPY IN UNCOMPLICATED MODERATE WASTING
subtopics.concept: For children aged 6–59 months with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) and uncomplicated moderate wasting, effective recovery through home diet therapy requires a quantified approach to meal frequency, energy density, and protein intake. WHO guidance suggests a total daily energy intake of approximately 100–130 kcal/kg/day during recovery, which should be framed as a “top-up” over the child’s usual intake. Practical bedside implementation involves prescribing a three-number plan to caregivers: first, the number of meals and snacks per day should be at least 5–6 feeds for children aged 6–24 months and “three meals plus two snacks” for children aged 2–5 years. Second, protein anchors should be included 2–3 times daily using affordable, locally available options such as dal, chana, rajma, egg, milk, curd, or soy, aligned with age-appropriate protein targets outlined in ICMR-NIN summaries. Third, energy density should be enhanced by adding calorie-dense ingredients like oil, ghee, groundnut, or til paste to each main meal after cooking. Food consistency is critical; meals should be thick enough to stay on a spoon, and low-energy liquids such as tea or thin gruels should be avoided as they suppress appetite. If milk is included, caregivers must be counseled against diluting it or using it as a replacement for solid foods. When supplementary rations are provided, they should be prescribed as an addition to family food rather than a replacement, and their timing should be planned as a snack or post-meal to minimize displacement of regular meals. Non-response to nutritional interventions is often due to inadequate intake rather than underlying medical issues. At follow-up visits, a detailed 24-hour dietary recall combined with portion estimation using household measures (e.g., katori, spoon, roti count) should be conducted to assess what was offered versus consumed, identify feeding practices (e.g., who fed the child, meal duration), and determine if supplements were shared or diluted. Based on this audit, caregivers and healthcare providers can agree on one feasible dietary change to implement over the next 14 days to optimize recovery.

subtopics.subtopic_title: AGE-BANDED FEEDING PRESCRIPTION FOR INFANTS 6 TO 11 MONTHS
subtopics.category: COUNSELLING AND HOME DIET THERAPY IN UNCOMPLICATED MODERATE WASTING
subtopics.concept: For infants aged 6 to 11 months with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM), feeding rehabilitation requires a structured approach to prevent rapid weight loss due to small deficits in meal frequency and energy density. National Infant and Young Child Feeding (IYCF) guidelines should serve as the foundation, but they must be translated into actionable plans that caregivers can implement immediately. For infants aged 6 to 8 months, breastfeeding on demand should continue, supplemented by 2 to 3 thick meals per day, with an additional snack if the infant’s appetite permits. Meals should begin with well-mashed foods, gradually increasing from a few spoonfuls to approximately half of a 250 mL cup over time. The food consistency should be thick enough to stay on a spoon, and energy density should be enhanced by mixing 1 teaspoon of oil or ghee into khichdi or porridge after cooking. For infants aged 9 to 11 months, breastfeeding should also continue, alongside 3 to 4 meals and 1 to 2 snacks daily. Foods can be mashed, finely chopped, or offered as safe finger foods, with a target of half a 250 mL bowl per meal and progressive increases in quantity. Practical options include thick khichdi with oil or ghee, soft roti pieces soaked in thick dal, curd or fruit as snacks, and culturally acceptable protein sources such as eggs or extra dal. Caregivers should be guided to make one small, sustainable change at each visit, such as adding a snack, stopping milk dilution, or incorporating oil into one main meal, as these incremental changes are more effective than complex diet charts. Responsive feeding practices should be encouraged, including sitting with the infant during meals, offering food patiently without force, and maintaining predictable mealtimes rather than continuous grazing. Caregivers should avoid low-energy liquids like tea, thin gruels, or watery health drinks, which suppress appetite without providing adequate calories. If complementary feeding raises caregiver concerns after diarrhoea, it is essential to explain that feeding should continue during illness, alongside appropriate rehydration and zinc supplementation when indicated.

case_studies.title: Moderate Acute Malnutrition in a 7-Month-Old Infant
case_studies.vignette: A 7-month-old male infant is brought to a rural primary health center in India by his mother, who reports that he has been losing weight over the past month despite breastfeeding. She mentions that he often refuses solid foods and eats only a few spoonfuls of mashed khichdi once or twice a day. The family has limited access to fresh vegetables and protein sources, relying primarily on rice and lentils. The infant has had intermittent fever over the past week but no diarrhea or vomiting. On examination, he appears thin and mildly irritable but is alert. His weight is 5.8 kg (previously 6.2 kg one month ago), and his mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) is 12.0 cm. His skin is intact, and there are no signs of edema. The mother expresses concern about his poor appetite and asks for guidance on feeding him better.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in infants aged 6 to 11 months and develop an age-appropriate supplementary feeding plan based on IYCF guidelines.

case_studies.title: Moderate Acute Malnutrition in a 7-Month-Old Infant
case_studies.vignette: A 7-month-old female infant is brought to a rural primary health center in India by her mother, who reports that the child has been lethargic and irritable for the past two weeks. The mother mentions that the infant is breastfed but has recently refused complementary foods, eating only 2–3 spoonfuls of diluted khichdi once or twice a day. The infant has no fever or diarrhea, but her weight gain has plateaued over the past month. On examination, the infant appears thin, with visible wasting in the arms and thighs. Her weight is 5.8 kg (below the WHO growth chart median for her age), and her mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) is measured at 12.1 cm. The child is alert but has a weak cry and poor appetite during feeding trials. The mother admits that she struggles to prepare energy-dense meals due to limited access to oil and ghee but is willing to learn how to improve the infant's diet. The family lives in a low-income setting where food insecurity is common.
case_studies.learning_objective: Apply age-banded feeding prescriptions to rehabilitate moderate acute malnutrition in infants aged 6 to 8 months using culturally appropriate, energy-dense foods and responsive feeding practices.

subtopics.subtopic_title: FEEDING PRESCRIPTION FOR CHILDREN 12 TO 59 MONTHS
subtopics.category: COUNSELLING AND HOME DIET THERAPY IN UNCOMPLICATED MODERATE WASTING
subtopics.concept: For children aged 12–59 months with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM), feeding prescriptions should be tailored to include meal frequency, energy density, and a feasible protein source, while considering caregiver capacity, time, and cost constraints. For children aged 12–23 months, breastfeeding should continue if already ongoing. These children should receive 3–4 meals and 1–2 snacks daily, with portions typically ranging from three-quarters to one full 250 mL cup per meal, depending on energy density. Instead of increasing portion sizes, energy density can be enhanced by adding a spoon of oil or ghee to one or two main meals daily and including a protein source such as an egg or an additional portion of dal or legumes. If milk is part of the diet, caregivers should be advised to use safe, full-strength milk without dilution to maintain calorie and protein density, ensuring milk does not replace solid foods. For children aged 24–59 months, family foods should be provided as 3 main meals and 2 snacks daily (“three-plus-two”). Energy density should be maintained by adding oil or ghee to each main meal, and watery foods that dilute calorie intake should be avoided. Low-nutrient grazing, such as tea, biscuits, or packaged snacks, should be replaced with planned, nutrient-rich snacks like bananas, roasted chana, curd, eggs, or legume-based options. Counselling should include documentation of a minimum dataset, including the problem statement, main barrier, one agreed-upon dietary change for 14 days, a meal and snack count, two specific foods the caregiver will provide daily, and a monitoring question for follow-up (e.g., “In the last 3 days, on how many days did the child finish the extra snack?”). If a supplementary ration is provided, its timing should be prescribed relative to meals, preferably after a main meal, to minimize displacement of home foods. Caregivers should also be asked to describe how the child is fed to ensure the plan is actionable and effective.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MICRONUTRIENT SUPPLEMENTATION AND INFECTION PREVENTION LINKAGES
subtopics.category: COUNSELLING AND HOME DIET THERAPY IN UNCOMPLICATED MODERATE WASTING
subtopics.concept: Micronutrient supplementation plays a critical role in infection prevention and recovery in paediatric patients with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM). Adjunct interventions should be integrated with India’s routine public health platforms rather than relying on costly private nutrition tonics. Micronutrient deficiencies and infection burdens often coexist in children with MAM, necessitating routine checks during outpatient visits to ensure age-appropriate prophylaxis and treatment through public programmes. Anaemia prevention is addressed under the Anaemia Mukt Bharat (AMB) initiative, which provides prophylactic iron-folic acid (IFA) supplementation. For children aged 6–59 months, IFA syrup is administered at 1 mL twice weekly, with each dose containing 20 mg elemental iron and 100 mcg folic acid. Children aged 5–10 years receive a weekly tablet containing 45 mg iron and 400 mcg folic acid, while those aged 10–19 years are given a weekly tablet containing 60 mg iron and 500 mcg folic acid. For children with clinical anaemia, treatment should follow severity-based national anaemia pathways rather than continuing prophylaxis alone. Deworming is conducted under National Deworming Day guidelines, with albendazole dosed as 200 mg for ages 1–2 years and 400 mg for ages 2–19 years, delivered through campaign platforms and frontline workers. Vitamin A prophylaxis is provided between 9 months and 5 years, with 100,000 IU administered at 9 months and 200,000 IU every six months thereafter, as per programme schedules. Zinc supplementation for acute diarrhoea follows WHO guidance, with 20 mg daily for 10–14 days or 10 mg daily for children under six months. During MAM visits, it is essential to verify immunization status and reinforce infection-prevention measures such as handwashing, safe water practices, and prompt treatment-seeking. Reducing infection recurrence is as vital as improving caloric intake for sustained recovery in paediatric patients with MAM. Practical bedside decision-making should focus on ensuring adherence to these public health protocols to optimize outcomes in this vulnerable population.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CATCH-UP IMMUNIZATION REFERRAL WORKFLOW DURING WASTING FOLLOW-UP
subtopics.category: COUNSELLING AND HOME DIET THERAPY IN UNCOMPLICATED MODERATE WASTING
subtopics.concept: Catch-up immunization during wasting follow-up is a critical component of managing moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in children, as it addresses immunization gaps that contribute to infection-driven weight loss. At every MAM visit, immunization status must be documented on day 0 and reassessed at the week 4 response node. In cases where the caregiver lacks an immunization card, the child should be treated as potentially underimmunized until records are verified through the nearest routine immunization session, facilitated by an ANM, ASHA, or facility register. The workflow for catch-up immunization begins with checking the child’s age and previously received vaccines, followed by identifying vaccines due under the National Immunization Schedule. Caregivers should be provided with a written note stating “acute wasting: prioritize routine immunization,” along with details of the nearest session location and date. Completion of vaccination should be verified at the next follow-up visit. Vaccination is recommended for clinically stable children with uncomplicated moderate wasting and should not be delayed solely due to the MAM label. It is important to emphasize that vaccine series do not require restarting; missed doses should be administered as catch-up following site-specific schedule guidance. Mild intercurrent illnesses are generally not contraindications to vaccination, and waiting for “perfect health” unnecessarily delays protection. For children from migrant families or those who frequently miss immunization sessions, proactive linkage to special drives such as Mission Indradhanush and community platforms like VHSND is essential. These linkages should be documented with details of where the family will go, when, and what will be checked at the next visit, similar to the documentation process for supplementary feeding. This structured approach transforms generic immunization advice into an actionable plan, reducing the risk of future diarrhoea and pneumonia episodes during recovery. Not specified in source: licensed vaccines (India), specific schedules, efficacy and waning, and contraindications.

case_studies.title: Catch-Up Immunization During Wasting Follow-Up
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy presents to the community health center in rural India for a follow-up visit after being identified with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) during a previous outreach camp. His mother reports that he has been eating better with the supplementary feeding program but has had intermittent low-grade fever for the past week. The child appears thin but alert, with a weight-for-height Z-score of -2.3 SD. On examination, mild pallor and wasting are noted, but there are no signs of dehydration, respiratory distress, or acute complications. The mother does not have the child’s immunization card and is unsure which vaccines he has received. The nearest routine immunization session is scheduled in three days at the village anganwadi center. The health worker provides the mother with a written referral note prioritizing catch-up immunization and explains the importance of completing the vaccine schedule. She documents the referral details in the child’s follow-up record and advises the mother to return in four weeks for reassessment, emphasizing the need to verify vaccination completion at that visit.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the workflow for catch-up immunization during MAM follow-up, including referral processes and prioritization of routine immunization for clinically stable children.

case_studies.title: Catch-Up Immunization Referral Workflow in a Child with Moderate Acute Malnutrition
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural village in Uttar Pradesh is brought to the local anganwadi center by his mother for follow-up after being diagnosed with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) two weeks ago. The child’s weight-for-height z-score was −2.5 SD at the initial visit. His mother reports no fever or diarrhea but mentions that he has been eating poorly and appears less active than usual. On examination, the boy is alert but thin, with visible wasting and a mid-upper arm circumference of 11.2 cm. His immunization card is missing, and the mother is unsure about his vaccination history. The anganwadi worker checks the National Immunization Schedule and notes that the child is likely due for measles and DPT booster doses. She provides the mother with a written referral to the nearest routine immunization session, scheduled in three days, and emphasizes the importance of completing catch-up vaccination. She documents the referral in the child’s MAM follow-up chart and asks the mother to return in two weeks with immunization records for verification.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the workflow for catch-up immunization during wasting follow-up and assess immunization status in moderately malnourished children.

subtopics.subtopic_title: USING ICDS ENTITLEMENTS AND COUNSELLING ON NUTRITION NORMS
subtopics.category: SUPPLEMENTARY FEEDING REGIMENS AND SAFE PRODUCT USE
subtopics.concept: In India, the Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS) and Mission Poshan provide the primary platform for supplementary feeding to address moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in children. These programmes aim to bridge the gap between recommended dietary allowances and actual intake through the supplementary nutrition programme, which operates for approximately 300 days per year via the anganwadi network. Nutrition is typically delivered as take-home rations (THR) or hot cooked meals (HCM), with entitlements linked to child registration. Mission Poshan guidelines specify daily nutrition norms for children aged 6–72 months, recommending about 500 kcal and 12–15 g of protein, while children classified as severely malnourished are advised higher targets of about 800 kcal and 20–25 g of protein. These norms, though subject to revision, serve as a clinical counselling framework. The 2017 supplementary nutrition rules under ICDS further outline enhanced provisions for malnourished children, including specific energy and protein targets for THR in younger children and additional calories and protein for older preschoolers, supplementing the standard meal. For bedside decision-making, it is essential to emphasize that supplementary nutrition is designed as a "top-up" to home diets, not a replacement. Caregivers should be counselled that home foods must continue to provide substantial energy and protein, as the ration alone is insufficient for full recovery. Lack of improvement in nutritional status may indicate issues such as inconsistent receipt of rations, sharing within the household, or improper preparation, rather than medical failure. For high-risk children receiving supplemental foods, aim for the supplementary energy contribution to constitute approximately 40–60% of total daily energy needs during recovery. Simultaneously, efforts should be made to protect the child’s intake of family foods to ensure that the supplement accelerates recovery rather than displacing regular meals. These practical considerations align with the WHO’s concept of supplementary feeding as a targeted intervention to support recovery in malnourished children.

subtopics.subtopic_title: HOUSEHOLD-LEVEL PRESCRIBING AND ADHERENCE AUDIT FOR THR AND HOT COOKED MEALS
subtopics.category: SUPPLEMENTARY FEEDING REGIMENS AND SAFE PRODUCT USE
subtopics.concept: For paediatric post-graduates, ensuring effective household-level prescribing and adherence to take-home rations (THR) and hot cooked meals (HCM) in managing moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is critical. These interventions should be operationalised like medications, rather than assuming that receiving the ration equates to its proper intake. Clinicians must first confirm the supply status, including the last pickup date, the number of days covered, and any stockouts. If daily attendance for HCM is impractical due to caregiver work or distance, the focus should shift to ensuring predictable THR access. In such cases, intensify the home diet prescription to maintain an energy-dense nutritional plan for the child. Caregivers should be explicitly informed that the ration is “the child’s medicine,” and a clear storage and serving plan should be agreed upon to minimise sharing. This plan may include using a separate container, fixed serving times, and caregiver supervision, with the agreement documented. Demonstrating preparation methods using the family’s utensils is essential, with emphasis on achieving a thick consistency and adding a fat source to enhance energy density. Caregivers should be cautioned against over-dilution into watery gruel or tea, which reduces caloric content and increases contamination risks. To prevent displacement of regular meals, THR should be prescribed as an additional snack or to follow meals, not as a replacement for lunch or dinner. During follow-up, a brief adherence audit should be conducted to assess days received, days consumed by the index child, sharing practices, storage conditions, and preparation errors. In cases of programme challenges, such as non-receipt, poor-quality packets, or repeated stockouts, an escalation ladder should be employed. This includes documenting issues with dates and quantities, providing a written linkage note to the Anganwadi Centre (AWC) supervisor or Child Development Project Officer (CDPO) highlighting clinical risks and the need for uninterrupted supplementary nutrition, and involving facility-based social support services if persistent failures jeopardise recovery. Not specified in source are details regarding prophylaxis/vaccination guidance, sanitation and food/water hygiene, return precautions, and community or outbreak reporting.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PRESCRIBABLE DOSING AND SAFE USE OF RUSF AND LNS
subtopics.category: SUPPLEMENTARY FEEDING REGIMENS AND SAFE PRODUCT USE
subtopics.concept: For the management of moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in children, specially formulated foods like ready-to-use supplementary food (RUSF) and lipid-based nutrient supplements (LNS) should be prescribed with the same precision as medications, specifying the name, dose, duration, and a plan for monitoring. Based on the WHO recovery target of 100–130 kcal/kg/day for total energy needs, supplementary feeding should contribute approximately 40–60% of this requirement, translating to 40–78 kcal/kg/day from the supplement alone. Practically, this means a 6 kg child requires 240–468 kcal/day, an 8 kg child needs 320–624 kcal/day, and a 10 kg child requires 400–780 kcal/day from the supplement. These figures represent the energy contribution from the supplement, not the total caloric intake. It is critical to note that RUSF and LNS are specifically designed for moderate wasting and should not be confused with ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF), which is intended for severe wasting or nutritional oedema. RUSF, as per UNICEF specifications, is consumed directly from the sachet without cooking or dilution, typically as one 100 g sachet per day providing approximately 535 kcal. It is not a substitute for breast milk. A defined course, usually 8–12 weeks, should be prescribed, with early reassessment for adherence and to monitor for potential displacement of home foods. Safe-use counselling is essential to prevent operational failures. Caregivers should be instructed to offer the supplement after the child has eaten home food or split it into two small portions given post-meals to minimize displacement. Sharing of the supplement should be discouraged, and a “child portion first” rule should be emphasized. Sachets must be stored sealed, away from heat and pests, and once opened, should not be stored for prolonged periods. Mixing the product with water should be avoided due to the risk of contamination. Screening for prior immediate food allergy reactions is necessary, and young children should be supervised while consuming thick pastes to reduce the risk of choking.

subtopics.subtopic_title: FOOD SAFETY COUNSELLING FOR THR AND HOT COOKED MEALS
subtopics.category: SUPPLEMENTARY FEEDING REGIMENS AND SAFE PRODUCT USE
subtopics.concept: Food safety counselling is a critical component of ensuring the success of supplementary feeding regimens for children with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM), particularly in the context of take-home rations (THR) and hot cooked meals (HCM). Unsafe food handling practices can lead to diarrhoea, which caregivers may mistakenly attribute to the nutritional intervention itself, undermining its effectiveness. For HCM, families should be advised to feed meals as fresh as possible and avoid leaving cooked food at room temperature for extended periods. If food is stored, it must be reheated thoroughly before feeding. For THR, caregivers should store ration packets in dry, covered containers, use clean and dry utensils, and ensure preparation with safe water. Handwashing with soap before food preparation and feeding is essential, as contaminated water and hands can transform nutritional support into a source of repeated diarrhoea. Rations should be discarded if they emit a foul smell, show visible insects, or exhibit moisture clumping. In cases where diarrhoea occurs, caregivers should be counselled to continue feeding and manage dehydration using standard low-osmolarity oral rehydration solution (ORS), unless severe wasting or oedema is present. Zinc supplementation should be provided for acute diarrhoea according to WHO guidelines: 20 mg daily for 10–14 days for children over six months, or 10 mg daily for those under six months. Framing rehydration and continued feeding as integral to the nutrition plan can help break the cycle of illness leading to reduced intake and further wasting. During follow-up visits, healthcare providers should audit food preparation practices by asking caregivers to demonstrate or describe how rations are prepared and consumed. If repeated diarrhoea is linked to unsafe practices or spoiled rations, it should be treated as a system and hygiene issue, with families guided to escalate concerns to the anganwadi supervisor for replacement. This approach safeguards clinical outcomes while maintaining caregiver trust in supplementary feeding programmes.

subtopics.subtopic_title: RISK-STRATIFIED FOLLOW-UP SCHEDULING AND RESPONSE ASSESSMENT
subtopics.category: FOLLOW-UP ALGORITHMS AND ESCALATION PATHWAYS
subtopics.concept: Risk-stratified follow-up scheduling and response assessment is central to the outpatient management of moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in children, as moderate wasting can rapidly worsen during intercurrent illnesses, and early non-response often signals correctable feeding or programmatic failures. A structured plan should be initiated on day 0, including documentation of baseline anthropometry (weight, length/height when feasible, oedema, and at least one of MUAC or WHZ/WLZ), noting the metric used to define eligibility, identifying dietary and health contributors, and linking the child to a reliable supplementary feeding pathway. Follow-up intensity should be tailored according to risk, using WHO criteria to classify children as high-risk or standard-risk. High-risk criteria include MUAC 115–119 mm, WAZ < -3, age < 24 months, relapse, comorbidities, or severe social adversity. In many outpatient Indian settings, high-risk children should ideally be reviewed within 7 days, while standard-risk children can be reviewed within 14 days, with earlier reviews warranted if acute illness arises. At follow-up, the primary focus is on the child’s trajectory: assessing whether the child is clinically well, eating better, and demonstrating consistent anthropometric improvement without red flags. If improvement is evident, the simplest sustainable plan should be reinforced, with follow-up visits spaced to fortnightly or monthly intervals in coordination with community monitoring. If there is no improvement, the response should not default to changing the supplementary feed; instead, re-check measurements, dietary intake, food sharing, and untreated illnesses, addressing identified failure points and intensifying follow-up. State models, as summarized in NITI Aayog and UNICEF compendia, typically recommend weekly or fortnightly visits during the active program phase (often up to 8 weeks), followed by monthly monitoring post-discharge. Aligning clinic notes with these time anchors ensures compatibility with frontline worker practices, facilitating effective continuity of care.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DAY ZERO WEEKLY AND MONTHLY DECISION NODES FOR OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP
subtopics.category: FOLLOW-UP ALGORITHMS AND ESCALATION PATHWAYS
subtopics.concept: In managing moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in paediatric outpatient settings, follow-up must be structured as dated decision nodes to promptly identify and address deterioration. Day 0 serves as the baseline assessment, where mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) and/or weight-for-height/length z-score (WHZ/WLZ) are confirmed, oedema is checked, appetite and danger signs are documented, and dietary or illness-related drivers are identified. On the same day, the child should be linked to supplementary feeding entitlements. High-risk classification is warranted if any WHO prioritization factor is present, such as MUAC 115–119 mm, weight-for-age z-score (WAZ) < -3, age under 24 months, relapse, prior severe acute malnutrition (SAM), comorbidities or disabilities, or severe social adversity. High-risk children require a follow-up review in 7 days, while others should be reviewed in 14 days. The first trajectory node occurs at Day 7 or Day 14, depending on the initial risk classification. At this point, anthropometry should be repeated with technique verification, and adherence to the feeding plan should be audited, including meals achieved, supplements received and consumed, dilution, and sharing. Any weight loss, falling MUAC, new oedema, poor appetite, or clinical deterioration necessitates immediate escalation, including repeat triage and referral or admission if complicated wasting is suspected. If there is no deterioration but no improvement in appetite or anthropometric trajectory, operational failures should be corrected first, and symptom-guided screening for underlying illnesses (e.g., persistent diarrhoea, urinary tract infection, tuberculosis, or anaemia) should be initiated before altering the feeding regimen. Week 4 marks the response node. If the child is clinically well, with improving appetite and upward anthropometric trends, the current plan should be continued, with follow-up spaced appropriately while maintaining programme linkage. However, non-response at Week 4 despite verified adherence necessitates staged investigations and possible referral, particularly when comorbidities or safeguarding concerns are present. By Week 8, persistent non-response is considered a failure of the outpatient pathway, requiring referral to a facility-based centre (e.g., NRC, FBMC, or paediatric ward) for comprehensive feeding and medical evaluation.

case_studies.title: Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM) Follow-Up Decision Node
case_studies.vignette: A 16-month-old boy from a rural village in India presents to the outpatient clinic on Day 0 for evaluation of poor weight gain over the last 2 months. His mother reports reduced appetite and frequent episodes of watery diarrhoea over the past three weeks. On examination, his weight-for-height z-score (WHZ) is -2.5, and his mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) measures 117 mm. There is no oedema, but he appears listless and mildly pale. His temperature is 37.4°C, and his respiratory rate is 34 breaths per minute. The mother mentions that the family has limited access to clean water and depends on seasonal agricultural work. The child has no history of severe acute malnutrition (SAM), but his weight-for-age z-score (WAZ) is -3.1. He is started on supplementary feeding and scheduled for a follow-up visit in 7 days. At the Day 7 review, his MUAC has dropped to 116 mm, and his weight has decreased by 200 grams. The mother reports difficulty adhering to the feeding plan due to food sharing within the household and the child’s persistent diarrhoea.
case_studies.learning_objective: Evaluate anthropometric trends, adherence to feeding plans, and clinical symptoms at follow-up decision nodes to identify deterioration or operational failures requiring escalation in MAM management.

subtopics.subtopic_title: VISIT CHECKLIST AND ESCALATION CRITERIA DURING FOLLOW-UP
subtopics.category: FOLLOW-UP ALGORITHMS AND ESCALATION PATHWAYS
subtopics.concept: During follow-up visits for children with Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM) undergoing supplementary feeding, a comprehensive checklist must be completed to identify deterioration or operational failures. Each visit should document anthropometric measurements, including weight, length/height when feasible, mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC), weight-for-height z-score (WHZ) or weight-for-length z-score (WLZ), with repeat measurements if borderline, and an oedema check. Appetite and feeding function should be assessed by asking if the child is eating more than during the previous visit, identifying who feeds the child, and noting the duration of meals. A morbidity screen must be conducted to check for diarrhoea, cough or breathing difficulty, fever, or danger signs. Additionally, a supplement use audit should evaluate the amount of ration or product received, consumed by the child, and any issues with sharing, storage, or preparation. The visit should conclude with a single agreed-upon change in care, a detailed plan for foods and feeding schedules until the next visit, and the date for the next follow-up. Escalation is critical when risks increase. Immediate referral or admission is warranted if nutritional oedema develops, any emergency or general danger sign appears, or clinical deterioration is observed. Weight loss or a declining MUAC during active follow-up should be treated as a red flag, prompting reassessment for untreated illness, inadequate intake, or unsafe home care. If complications are suspected, referral for higher-level assessment is necessary. Indian protocols, as outlined in national compendia, specifically use complications or weight loss to trigger referral to facility-based centres like Nutrition Rehabilitation Centres (NRC). In cases where specially formulated foods are unavailable, WHO high-risk criteria should guide more frequent follow-ups and a lower threshold for investigation and referral, rather than reduced intervention. This structured approach ensures timely identification of at-risk children and facilitates appropriate escalation of care to improve outcomes in MAM management.

case_studies.title: Follow-Up Visit for Moderate Acute Malnutrition in a 2-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy from a rural village in India presents for his third follow-up visit at a community health center after being enrolled in a supplementary feeding program for Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM) three weeks ago. His mother reports that he has been eating slightly better but still takes a long time to finish meals. She mentions that he had a fever two days ago and has been coughing at night. On examination, the child appears lethargic but responsive. His weight has decreased by 200 grams since the last visit, and his mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) is now 11.2 cm, compared to 11.5 cm previously. No nutritional oedema is noted. The mother states that the supplementary food ration is being shared with his siblings due to food scarcity at home. The child’s temperature is 38.5°C, and his respiratory rate is 46 breaths per minute. No other danger signs are present. The health worker completes the checklist, noting the decline in weight and MUAC, the recent fever, and the suboptimal use of the supplementary ration.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize red flags during follow-up visits for Moderate Acute Malnutrition, including weight loss, declining MUAC, fever, and operational challenges, and determine when escalation or referral is necessary.

subtopics.subtopic_title: REFERRAL CRITERIA AND HANDOVER DOCUMENTATION FOR ESCALATION TO FACILITY CARE
subtopics.category: FOLLOW-UP ALGORITHMS AND ESCALATION PATHWAYS
subtopics.concept: Escalation from outpatient management of moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) to facility-based care must be prompt and clearly defined to prevent delays in addressing deterioration. Immediate referral is warranted if bilateral pitting oedema develops, severe wasting thresholds are reached (MUAC <115 mm and/or WHZ/WLZ <-3), or appetite is poor upon clinical assessment. Additionally, any complication requiring facility care necessitates escalation, including persistent vomiting, severe dehydration or shock, hypothermia, high fever with toxicity, respiratory distress, severe palmar pallor, extensive skin or eye lesions, or situations where home care is deemed unsafe. In India, children meeting these criteria should be referred to the nearest Nutrition Rehabilitation Centre (NRC) or paediatric ward following Facility-Based Management of Children (FBMC) operational guidelines for stabilization and nutritional rehabilitation. Post-stabilization, continuity of care should involve supplementary feeding and monitoring through community pathways, such as ICDS entitlements via Anganwadi Centres (AWCs) and CMAM workflows where available. Initial laboratory investigations are not specified in the source. Stabilization steps prior to referral include preventing hypoglycaemia with urgent feeding if safe, keeping the child warm, treating dehydration cautiously, and administering first-dose antibiotics only if a clear severe infection syndrome is identified and local protocols permit without delaying referral. Severe dehydration requiring cautious rehydration or other critical signs may necessitate escalation to a paediatric intensive care unit (PICU), though specific thresholds for PICU admission are not detailed in the source. A comprehensive handover note is essential to prevent information loss during referral. This should include dated anthropometric measurements (weight, length/height if available, MUAC, oedema), appetite status, feeding dysfunction observations (e.g., cough or choking with feeds, prolonged meals), the reason for referral, treatments already administered, details of supplementary feeding exposure (e.g., THR/HCM or products received and consumed), and a brief social risk assessment (e.g., food insecurity, caregiver capacity, safeguarding concerns). Discharge and review triggers are not specified in the source.

case_studies.title: Referral Criteria for Escalation in Moderate Acute Malnutrition
case_studies.vignette: A 17-month-old girl from a rural village in India is brought to the Anganwadi Centre by her mother due to poor appetite and lethargy over the past week. She has a history of moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) and has been enrolled in supplementary feeding programs for the past three months. Despite initial weight gain, her mother reports that she has progressively worsened over the last 10 days, refusing meals and experiencing intermittent vomiting. On examination, the child appears weak and irritable. Her mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) measures 113 mm, and bilateral pitting oedema is noted in her lower limbs. She has a high-grade fever (39.2°C) and severe palmar pallor. Respiratory rate is 48 breaths/min, and mild intercostal retractions are observed. The Anganwadi worker suspects deterioration and contacts the nearest primary health centre for urgent referral. A handover note is prepared, documenting the child’s clinical findings, nutritional history, and recent symptoms, along with urgent recommendations for facility-based management.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the criteria for referral and appropriate handover documentation for escalation of moderate acute malnutrition to facility-based care.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SUSTAINED RECOVERY CRITERIA AND DISCHARGE PLANNING
subtopics.category: DISCHARGE CRITERIA AND RELAPSE PREVENTION AFTER RECOVERY
subtopics.concept: Sustained recovery criteria and discharge planning for children treated for moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) must emphasize stability and continuity of care. According to the 2023 WHO wasting guideline, percentage or absolute weight gain should not be used as outpatient exit criteria. Instead, discharge should be based on sustained anthropometric recovery. A conservative and clinic-safe definition of recovery includes the child being clinically well, eating adequately, free from nutritional oedema, and demonstrating sustained anthropometric recovery across consecutive measurements. When possible, dual criteria should be used to minimize the risk of discharging a child based on one metric while they remain eligible by another. Recovery should be defined as a mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) of ≥125 mm and a weight-for-height z-score (WHZ) or weight-for-length z-score (WLZ) of ≥ -2, both maintained for at least two consecutive visits, with no bilateral pitting oedema observed during these visits. If only one metric can be reliably measured, discharge can be based on that metric, but the limitation must be documented, and a lower threshold for continued monitoring should be maintained, particularly for high-risk children. In Indian operational workflows, WHZ combined with oedema is often prioritized for community screening and programme entry. To ensure continuity across migration and referrals, both MUAC and WHZ/WLZ should be recorded at discharge whenever possible, with explicit documentation of which metric was used for programme entry and clinical monitoring. Discharge from clinic-led active wasting follow-up must include a clear linkage plan for ongoing community-based growth monitoring, such as through Anganwadi Centres (AWC) or Village Health, Sanitation, and Nutrition Days (VHSND), rather than leaving follow-up open-ended. Before discharge, it is essential to confirm that the feeding plan is practical, caregivers understand how to use any provided ration, and the next monitoring touchpoint is scheduled, whether through clinic review or community monitoring. These measures help ensure sustained recovery and reduce the risk of relapse.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DEFINING TREATMENT FAILURE RELAPSE AND POST- RECOVERY MONITORING
subtopics.category: DISCHARGE CRITERIA AND RELAPSE PREVENTION AFTER RECOVERY
subtopics.concept: Defining treatment failure, relapse, and post-recovery monitoring in paediatric moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is essential for guiding follow-up care and decision-making. Expected defervescence in MAM management involves early improvement within 1–2 weeks, marked by clinical stabilization and upward anthropometric trends. Treatment failure is suspected in cases of early deterioration, such as new emergency or danger signs or anthropometric worsening (e.g., weight loss or falling MUAC), which necessitate urgent illness management, repeat triage, and referral or admission if complications are present. Non-response at structured follow-up, typically after two visits within 2–4 weeks, is defined as the absence of meaningful improvement in appetite, feeding behavior, or anthropometric trajectory despite verified adherence to the feeding and supplementation plan. This should prompt an operational audit to investigate potential errors in measurement techniques, sharing, displacement, or preparation, followed by a targeted medical evaluation rather than immediate product changes. Persistent non-response by 8 weeks, as per some Indian state protocols, serves as a practical threshold for referral to a higher facility for comprehensive assessment. Relapse is clinically significant, as it often indicates unresolved household food insecurity, recurrent infections, or unaddressed comorbidities. Indian facility-based malnutrition guidance operationally defines relapse as re-eligibility for MAM criteria within approximately 2 months after cure. Specifically, relapse is characterized by moderate wasting (MUAC 115–124 mm and/or WHZ/WLZ -3 to -2) or clinical deterioration suggesting risk of progression. Post-recovery monitoring should follow a graduated schedule tailored to service touchpoints. This includes one review within the first month after recovery, preferably through VHSND or AWC growth monitoring, monthly reviews during months 2–3 for high-risk children, and at least one additional review between months 4–6 for children under 24 months or those with major social vulnerabilities. These measures aim to ensure sustained recovery and early detection of relapse or treatment failure. Not specified in the source are details regarding switching or extending therapy.

subtopics.subtopic_title: RESPONSIVE CAREGIVING AND EARLY LEARNING COUNSELLING DURING RECOVERY MONITORING
subtopics.category: DISCHARGE CRITERIA AND RELAPSE PREVENTION AFTER RECOVERY
subtopics.concept: Responsive caregiving and early learning counselling during recovery monitoring are critical components in the management of moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) to ensure sustained nutritional recovery and prevent relapse. Nutrition recovery is influenced not only by what children are fed but also by how they are fed. Counselling should emphasize responsive caregiving, where caregivers decide what foods to offer and when, while the child determines how much to eat. Active and patient feeding practices, such as sitting with the child, maintaining eye contact, and using gentle prompts, should be encouraged. Coercion, distractions like screens, or prolonged struggles during feeding should be avoided, as these can reduce intake and increase caregiver stress. A practical script for busy clinics includes: “Sit the child upright and close. Offer thick food slowly. Watch for cues; if the child refuses, pause and try again, but do not force. Praise any attempt to eat.” For children with low appetite following illness, smaller, more frequent meals should be recommended, along with reassurance that appetite will improve as infections resolve. Psychosocial stimulation is another essential aspect of recovery and relapse prevention. Caregivers should be advised to incorporate “play and talk” routines into meals and daily care, such as naming objects, singing, and engaging in simple games. Toddlers can be encouraged to self-feed with finger foods to promote autonomy and improve intake, while preschoolers benefit from consistent routines and reduced screen time to support appetite regulation and sleep. When caregiving challenges such as maternal depression, severe family stress, substance use, or neglect are identified, these should be treated as clinical risk factors for non-response and relapse. In such cases, families should be linked to community support systems, including ICDS home visits, Anganwadi Centre (AWC) platforms, and maternal mental health or social services. It is essential to document one achievable caregiving action during each visit for review at the next follow-up, similar to documenting dietary changes, to ensure progress is monitored effectively.
